stocks.note: Numbers as of market close April 17th.
stocks.news:
Netflix (NFLX -9.72%) got canceled on Friday, shedding ~10% in its worst session in six months. Q1 sales beat at $12.25B, but a $2.8B Warner Bros. Breakup check did most of the heavy lifting. Strip that out and earnings miss. Q2 guidance came in light and co-founder Reed Hastings exits the board in June. A beat that needs a breakup check is still a miss.

Quantum made a leap. IonQ (IONQ +3.26%) and D-Wave (QBTS +0.79%) each ripped about 50% on the week after Nvidia (NVDA +1.68%) dropped free software called Ising that cuts quantum setup time from days to hours. IonQ also linked two remote machines and landed a Pentagon contract. The whole space got a shot of adrenaline.

economy.note: US 10-Year and Gold as of 10:40 PM ET April 19th; WTI Crude reflects today's intraday move.
economy.commentary:
Lowe's (LOW +3.84%) put $250M behind a bet that AI can't climb a ladder. A decade-long plan to recruit and train 250,000 plumbers, carpenters, and electricians. The backdrop: construction firms need 349,000 trade workers by year-end, 92% can't find help, and 47% of skilled tradespeople already out-earn the average college grad. Real wages for real work.

March CPI came in hot at +0.9% monthly. The biggest jump in a while. But gas did almost all the damage at +21.2% on Iran fallout. Strip food and energy and core rose just +0.2%, below forecasts. The sticker shock is narrow (the pump), not broad-based. Which is why the Fed probably keeps its cool.

WTI crude snapped back above $87 after Iran vowed to retaliate for a seized tanker. The Strait of Hormuz story had five plot twists in four days: open Thursday, closed Friday, back open Saturday, US seizure Sunday. Volatility at the pump is the one thing you can count on.

crypto.commentary:
Bitcoin bounced, then cooled. BTC printed a two-month high near $78,384 last week, powered by the US-Iran ceasefire, a reopened Hormuz, fresh US crypto rules, oil down 11%, and $815M in shorts liquidated on the way up. American Bitcoin Corp (ABTC +15.04%) led the run.

Saylor's entire stack is now green. Strategy (MSTR +11.80%) sits on 780,897 BTC at an average cost of $75,577. Its first paper profit since early January. He kept buying through the crash, added $1B more at $71,902, and still wants 1 million coins by year-end. Conviction you can measure in billions.

real_estate.homebuilder: Ceasefire trimmed mortgage rates. The 30-year slid from 6.37% to 6.30% as the US–Iran truce pulled the 10-year Treasury yield lower. Fragile setup: one bad headline out of Tehran and it flips right back.
real_estate.commentary:
Across our 15-city tracker, active inventory hit 2,714 listings today. Up roughly 5-7% over last week in most cities as spring supply finally shows up. Alameda and Santa Clara counties are also reporting longer days on market, a sign the buyer pool is moving more cautiously even with rates easing. More to show, fewer ready to act. Sharper pricing and prep matter more than ever.

📍 Fremont: 339 total · 34 new. 46 TH active (16 pending), 78 CO active (16 pending), 108 DU/DE/PH active (75 pending). Detached still drives the pending column.

📍 Oakland: 756 total · 60 new. The Bay's deepest pool, and it just got deeper.

📍 Hayward: 227 total · 19 new. Fresh listings ticking up.

📍 Livermore: 213 total · 27 new. Biggest week-over-week jump in the Tri-Valley.

📍 San Ramon: 183 total · 28 new. Pipeline stays full.

📍 Danville: 179 total · 30 new. New listings strong, total holding.

📍 Dublin: 168 total · 30 new.

📍 Pleasanton: 132 total · 20 new.

📍 Milpitas: 118 total · 4 new. CS pipeline is loaded, fresh supply still light.

📍 Newark: 114 total · 7 new.

📍 San Leandro: 92 total · 13 new.

📍 Union City: 86 total · 5 new. Still one of the tightest markets on the board.

📍 Castro Valley: 77 total · 13 new.

📍 San Lorenzo: 24 total · 5 new.

📍 Sunol: 6 total · 0 new.

Office fire sale: A Chicago tower that traded for $68M in 2016 just sold for $4.2M. A 94% haircut, not a typo. In Denver, a complex once valued at $176M went for $5.3M at auction. 204 distressed office deals closed last year, up from 133 the year before. One of the Chicago towers is being converted to apartments. Often the only math that pencils at those prices.

Build-to-rent retreat: Single-family build-to-rent starts fell 19% year-over-year. From 84,000 units in 2024 to 68,000 in 2025. Financing costs climbed while rents rose just 1.2%, and the math stopped working for investors. Another small tailwind for resale inventory.
